cards.back: Layer-2 loops in switched topologies with redundant links. Without STP, a broadcast or unknown unicast loops forever, MAC tables churn, and the network melts down (broadcast storm). STP blocks redundant paths, leaving a loop-free tree, and re-enables them on failure.

cards.front: What 3 problems does an L2 loop cause?
cards.back: (1) Broadcast storms — broadcasts circulate forever, saturating links. (2) MAC address table instability — switches see the same MAC on multiple ports, constantly reflashing. (3) Multiple frame copies — receivers get duplicates, breaking applications.

cards.front: What is the bridge ID and how is the root bridge elected?
cards.back: Bridge ID = priority (4096 increments × extended system ID with VLAN) + MAC address. Switches exchange BPDUs; the lowest BID wins root election. Default priority 32768, modified to 32768+VLAN_ID by extended system ID. To force a switch to be root: lower its priority (`spanning-tree vlan X priority 4096`).

cards.front: Name the 3 STP port roles and what each does in steady state.
cards.back: Root port (RP): one per non-root switch — best path back to the root. Designated port (DP): one per segment (link) — forwards traffic on that segment; root has all DPs. Blocking/Alternate port: any non-root, non-designated port — receives BPDUs but doesn't forward data, prevents loops.

cards.front: Name the 5 classic STP port states (802.1D) in transition order.
cards.back: Disabled (admin down) → Blocking (only listens to BPDUs) → Listening (15s) → Learning (15s) → Forwarding. Total convergence 30-50s. RSTP collapses to: Discarding, Learning, Forwarding.

cards.front: How is the root port chosen when multiple paths exist?
cards.back: Tiebreakers in order: (1) Lowest cumulative path cost to root. (2) Lowest bridge ID of advertising neighbor. (3) Lowest sender port ID. (4) Lowest local port priority/ID. Cost is per-link based on speed (10 Gbps=2, 1 Gbps=4, 100 Mbps=19, 10 Mbps=100 — long path cost differs).

cards.front: What's the difference between PVST+ and standard 802.1D?
cards.back: Cisco PVST+ runs an independent STP instance per VLAN, allowing per-VLAN root and load-sharing across redundant trunks. Default on Cisco switches. 802.1D is single-instance (one STP for the whole bridged network); MST groups multiple VLANs into one instance.

cards.front: What happens to convergence time when a link fails in classic 802.1D STP?
cards.back: Up to 50 seconds: 20s max-age + 15s listening + 15s learning. Way too slow for modern networks — which is why RSTP (sub-second convergence with proposal/agreement and edge ports) is standard now.

cards.front: What does `show spanning-tree` (or `show spanning-tree vlan X`) reveal?
cards.back: Per-VLAN: root bridge ID + cost + port, this switch's bridge ID, hello/forward-delay/max-age timers, then per-port: role (Root/Desg/Altn), state (FWD/BLK/DIS), cost, priority/portID. The first thing to check when diagnosing root election issues.
